title: Body Parts & Health Vocabulary
description: Learn essential Czech vocabulary for body parts and common health terms — from hlava (head) to noha (leg) — and describe basic physical conditions and ailments.

exercises.prompt: Match each body part to its English translation.

data.prompt_en: Match each Czech body part to its English meaning.
data.explanation: Basic body parts: 'hlava' = head, 'ruka' = hand or arm (Czech uses one word for both), 'noha' = leg or foot (also one word), 'oko' = eye. Note that Czech 'ruka' and 'noha' cover what English splits into two words each.

exercises.prompt: Match each health-related term to its meaning.

data.prompt_en: Match each Czech health term to its English meaning.
data.explanation: Health vocabulary: 'bolí' = hurts (3rd person singular of 'bolet'), 'nemoc' = illness, 'lékař' = doctor (masculine), 'lek' = medicine/drug. 'Bolí mě hlava' = My head hurts.
'Bolí mě břicho' = My stomach hurts. 'Břicho' = stomach/belly. The structure is 'bolí mě + body part' — the verb 'bolet' (to hurt) is impersonal, always 3rd person singular.
'Zub' = tooth. Other body parts: 'nos' = nose, 'ucho' = ear, 'krk' = neck/throat. A dentist is 'zubní lékař' (tooth doctor).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the body part: 'My head hurts.'

data.explanation: 'Bolí mě hlava.' = My head hurts. 'Hlava' = head. The structure 'bolí mě + [body part in nominative]' is the standard way to express pain in Czech.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct word: 'I am sick.' — 'Jsem ____.'

data.explanation: 'Jsem nemocný.' = I am sick (masculine). 'Jsem nemocná.' = I am sick (feminine). The adjective 'nemocný/nemocná' agrees with the speaker's gender. 'Nemoc' is the noun (illness), 'nemocný' is the adjective (sick/ill).

data.grammar_note: 'Bolí mě krk.' = My throat hurts. 'Krk' = neck/throat. The impersonal verb 'bolí' (hurts) + 'mě' (me) + body part. Alternatively: 'Bolí mě v krku' (It hurts in my throat) using the locative case.
'Mám kašel' = I have a cough. 'Kašel' = cough (masculine noun). The verb 'mít' (to have) + noun is used to describe symptoms, similar to English.

exercises.prompt: Listen and write what you hear.

data.note: 'Záda' = back (plural noun in Czech, always plural). 'Bolí mě záda' = My back hurts.

exercises.prompt: Arrange the words to form: 'I have a fever.'

data.explanation: 'Mám horečku.' = I have a fever. The correct sentence uses 'Mám' (I have) + 'horečku' (fever - accusative). 'Teplotu' (temperature) is a distractor — the more specific word for fever is 'horečka'. Alternative: 'Mám teplotu' (I have a temperature) is also valid.

exercises.prompt: Find the mistake in this sentence.

data.prompt_en: Find the mistake in this health-related sentence.
data.explanation: Both forms can be correct depending on context. 'Bolí mě zub' = My (one) tooth hurts. 'Bolí mě zuby' = My teeth hurt (plural). If only one tooth hurts, use singular 'zub'. The verb 'bolí' stays the same for both singular and plural subjects since it's impersonal.
